---
title: "ACE Disparity Brief rundown"
author: "Caleb"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_document: default
  pdf_document: default
editor_options: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 90
---


## First key enquiry: how accurate is ancestral state estimation?

There are three different parameters here:

* transition rate
* fossil sampling level
* method of collapsing ancestral states.

I measured the Hamming distance between true and 'aced' estimations. 

This gives a measure of the accuracy of raw ace.


```{R echo = FALSE}
library(knitr)
## load the hamming data
relative_majority_rule <- readRDS("../Data/matrices/pointstrictmeans.rds")
no_majority_rule <- readRDS("../Data/matrices/samplestrictmeans.rds")
strict_majority_rule <- readRDS("../Data/matrices/threshstrictmeans.rds")

########################################################################################################################

## Load the disparity data
relvardiffs <- readRDS("../Data/matrices/discrete/relvardiffs.rds")
samplevardiffs <- readRDS("../Data/matrices/discrete/samplevardiffs.rds")
strictvardiffs <- readRDS("../Data/matrices/discrete/strictvardiffs.rds")

########################################################################################################################


```

```{R echo = FALSE, , warning = FALSE, fig.width=12, fig.height=8, out.width="100%"}

levels <- c("fast", "med", "slow")

# Loop through each level and generate a plot
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)


# Combine data for all levels and replicates into a single data frame
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)

# Combine strict_means and strict_means_sample into a single data frame
combined_data <- do.call(rbind, lapply(c("strict_majority_rule", "relative_majority_rule", "no_majority_rule"), function(dataset_name) {
  dataset <- get(dataset_name)  # Dynamically get the dataset by name
  do.call(rbind, lapply(names(dataset), function(level) {
    do.call(rbind, lapply(seq_along(dataset[[level]]), function(replicate_index) {
      data <- dataset[[level]][[replicate_index]]
      df <- data.frame(
        replicate = replicate_index,
        all = data$all,
        high = data$fossil_high,
        mid = data$fossil_med,
        low = data$fossil_low,
        living = data$living,
        level = level,
        threshold_method = dataset_name  # Add a column for treatment
      )
      df_long <- pivot_longer(df, cols = c("living", "low", "mid", "high", "all"),
                              names_to = "preservation_level", values_to = "distance")
      df_long$preservation_level <- factor(df_long$preservation_level, levels = c("living", "low", "mid", "high", "all"))
      return(df_long)
    }))
  }))
}))
combined_data$threshold_method <- factor(combined_data$threshold_method, levels = c("strict_majority_rule", "relative_majority_rule", "no_majority_rule"),labels = c("Strict majority rule", "Relative majority rule", "No majority rule (sample)"))


# png("../Manuscript/figures/strictdistance.png", width = 350, height = 250, units = "mm", res = 600)
colors <- c("slow" = "#8805A8", "med" = "#00B945", "fast" = "#FFB600")

# Generate the combined plot with faceting by threshold_method

colors <- c("slow" = "#8805A8", "med" = "#00B945", "fast" = "#FFB600")

# Generate the combined plot with violin plots
p <- ggplot(combined_data, aes(x = preservation_level, y = distance, color = level, fill = level)) +
  geom_boxplot(
    alpha = 0.7,
    outlier.size = 0.5,
    outlier.alpha = 0.6,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.8)
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ threshold_method, ncol = 3) +
  labs(
    x = "Fossil sampling",
    y = "Mean Hamming distance score",
    color = "Transition Rate",
    fill = "Transition Rate"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_color_manual(values = colors) +  # Apply custom colors to lines
  scale_fill_manual(values = colors) +   # Apply custom colors to ribbons
  theme(
    # Thicker axis labels and ticks
    axis.text = element_text(size = 15, face = "bold"),      # Larger, bold axis labels
    axis.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),     # Larger, bold axis titles
    axis.ticks = element_line(size = 1),                   # Thicker axis ticks
    
    # # Legend
    # legend.position = "right",                 
    # legend.title = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"),
    # legend.text = element_text(size = 10),
    legend.position = "none",
    
    panel.grid = element_blank(),             
    
    # Facet labels
    strip.text = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5, vjust = 1),
    strip.background = element_rect(color = "black", fill = "white", linewidth = 0.5),
    panel.border = element_rect(color = "black", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.5)
  ) +
  scale_y_reverse(limits = c(1.0, 0.0), expand = c(0,0))  # Ensure 0 is included

ggsave("../Manuscript/figures/rawdist.png", p, width = 12, height = 8, dpi = 700, bg = "white")


```

This figure can go into the supplementary, as it does not ask the main question relating to disparity...


From these results:

* faster transition rates = worse accuracy
* sample method (probabilistic) is superior to strict/rel methods (deterministic)
* in general, higher fossil sampling improves accuracy



## Sum of variances

So we know that more fossils and lower rates increases accuracy of raw ancestral states

Now lets see how that translates to disparity?

In this case, we use a size-based metric (sum of variances)


```{R echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, fig.width=14, fig.height=5, out.width="100%"}

# discrete_var_default_diffs <- readRDS("../Data/matrices/pointvardiffs.rds")
# discrete_var_sample_diffs <- readRDS("../Data/matrices/samplevardiffs.rds")
# discrete_var_thresh_diffs <- readRDS("../Data/matrices/threshvardiffs.rds")
disp_diff_strict <- readRDS("../Data/cluster/discrete/dispdiffs/disp_diff_strict.rds")
disp_diff_sample <- readRDS("../Data/cluster/discrete/dispdiffs/disp_diff_sample.rds")
disp_diff_rel <- readRDS("../Data/cluster/discrete/dispdiffs/disp_diff_rel.rds")
disp_diff_no_ace <- readRDS("../Data/cluster/discrete/dispdiffs/disp_diff_no_ace.rds")
post_ord_diffs <- readRDS("../Data/matrices/discrete/post_ord_ace.Rds")

library(scales)
combined_data <- do.call(rbind, lapply(c("disp_diff_strict", "disp_diff_rel", "disp_diff_sample", "disp_diff_no_ace"), function(dataset_name) {
  dataset <- get(dataset_name)  # Dynamically get the dataset by name
  do.call(rbind, lapply(names(dataset), function(level) {
    do.call(rbind, lapply(seq_along(dataset[[level]]), function(replicate_index) {
      data <- dataset[[level]][[replicate_index]]
      df <- data.frame(
        replicate = replicate_index,
        all = data$all,
        high = data$fossil_high,
        mid = data$fossil_med,
        low = data$fossil_low,
        living = data$living,
        level = level,
        threshold_method = dataset_name  # Add a column for treatment
      )
      df_long <- pivot_longer(df, cols = c("living", "low", "mid", "high", "all"),
                              names_to = "preservation_level", values_to = "distance")
      df_long$preservation_level <- factor(df_long$preservation_level, levels = c("living", "low", "mid", "high", "all"))
      return(df_long)
    }))
  }))
}))

combined_data$threshold_method <- factor(combined_data$threshold_method, levels = c("disp_diff_strict", "disp_diff_rel", "disp_diff_sample", "disp_diff_no_ace"), labels = c("Strict majority rule", "Relative majority rule", "No majority rule (sample)", "No ACE"))

# log10_reverse_trans <- trans_new(
#   name = "log10_reverse",
#   transform = function(x) -log10(x),  # Apply log10 and reverse
#   inverse = function(x) 10^(-x)      # Inverse of the transformation
# )

colors <- c("slow" = "#8805A8", "med" = "#00B945", "fast" = "#FFB600")
# Generate the combined plot

p <- ggplot(combined_data, aes(x = preservation_level, y = distance, color = level, fill = level)) +
  geom_boxplot(
    alpha = 0.7,
    outlier.size = 0.5,
    outlier.alpha = 0.6,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.8)
  ) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "black", linewidth = 0.6, linetype = "dashed") +
  facet_wrap(~ threshold_method, ncol = 4, nrow = 1) +
  labs(
    x = "Fossil sampling",
    y = "Relative disparity error",
    color = "Transition Rate",
    fill = "Transition Rate"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_color_manual(values = colors) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = colors) + 
  theme(
    axis.text = element_text(size = 15, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.ticks = element_line(size = 1),
    legend.position = "none",
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    strip.text = element_text(size = 15, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5, vjust = 1),
    strip.background = element_rect(color = "black", fill = "white", linewidth = 0.5),
    panel.border = element_rect(color = "black", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.5)
  ) +
  coord_trans(y = "pseudo_log") +
  scale_y_reverse()

ggsave("../Manuscript/figures/sumvar.png", p, width = 12, height = 8, dpi = 700, bg = "white")


```

Key takeaways:

* Interestingly, disparity is recovered well, even when the raw accuracy (see earlier figure) is poor (i.e. fast rates)
* Again, the sample method outperforms rel/strict methods.
* Interestingly, performing ace post-ordination also performs well! It will be interesting to see whether this is true for different disparity metrics...


## Average nearest neighbour (density)

Here I looked at a density metric (average nearest neighbour)

```{R echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, fig.width=14, fig.height=5, out.width="100%"}

# discrete_var_default_diffs <- readRDS("../Data/matrices/pointvardiffs.rds")
# discrete_var_sample_diffs <- readRDS("../Data/matrices/samplevardiffs.rds")
# discrete_var_thresh_diffs <- readRDS("../Data/matrices/threshvardiffs.rds")
neighbours_rel_diff <- readRDS( "../Data/matrices/discrete/neighbours.rel.diff.Rds")
neighbours_sample_diff <-  readRDS("../Data/matrices/discrete/neighbours.sample.diff.Rds")
neighbours_strict_diff <-  readRDS("../Data/matrices/discrete/neighbours.strict.diff.Rds")
neighbours_no_ace_diff <- readRDS("../Data/matrices/discrete/neighbours.noace.diff.Rds")


library(scales)
combined_data <- do.call(rbind, lapply(c("neighbours_strict_diff", "neighbours_rel_diff", "neighbours_sample_diff", "neighbours_no_ace_diff"), function(dataset_name) {
  dataset <- get(dataset_name)  # Dynamically get the dataset by name
  do.call(rbind, lapply(names(dataset), function(level) {
    do.call(rbind, lapply(seq_along(dataset[[level]]), function(replicate_index) {
      data <- dataset[[level]][[replicate_index]]
      df <- data.frame(
        replicate = replicate_index,
        all = data$all,
        high = data$fossil_high,
        mid = data$fossil_med,
        low = data$fossil_low,
        living = data$living,
        level = level,
        threshold_method = dataset_name  # Add a column for treatment
      )
      df_long <- pivot_longer(df, cols = c("living", "low", "mid", "high", "all"),
                              names_to = "preservation_level", values_to = "distance")
      df_long$preservation_level <- factor(df_long$preservation_level, levels = c("living", "low", "mid", "high", "all"))
      return(df_long)
    }))
  }))
}))

combined_data$threshold_method <- factor(combined_data$threshold_method, levels = c("neighbours_strict_diff", "neighbours_rel_diff", "neighbours_sample_diff", "neighbours_no_ace_diff"), labels = c("Strict majority rule", "Relative majority rule", "No majority rule (sample)", "No ACE"))

# log10_reverse_trans <- trans_new(
#   name = "log10_reverse",
#   transform = function(x) -log10(x),  # Apply log10 and reverse
#   inverse = function(x) 10^(-x)      # Inverse of the transformation
# )

colors <- c("slow" = "#8805A8", "med" = "#00B945", "fast" = "#FFB600")
# Generate the combined plot
p <- ggplot(combined_data, aes(x = preservation_level, y = distance, color = level, fill = level)) +
  geom_boxplot(
    alpha = 0.7,
    outlier.size = 0.5,
    outlier.alpha = 0.6,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.8)
  ) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "black", linewidth = 0.6, linetype = "dashed") +
  facet_wrap(~ threshold_method, ncol = 4, nrow = 1) +
  labs(
    x = "Fossil sampling",
    y = "Relative disparity error",
    color = "Transition Rate",
    fill = "Transition Rate"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_color_manual(values = colors) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = colors) + 
  theme(
    axis.text = element_text(size = 15, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.ticks = element_line(size = 1),
    legend.position = "none",
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    strip.text = element_text(size = 15, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5, vjust = 1),
    strip.background = element_rect(color = "black", fill = "white", linewidth = 0.5),
    panel.border = element_rect(color = "black", fill = NA, linewidth = 0.5)
  ) +
  coord_trans(y = "pseudo_log") +
  scale_y_reverse()

ggsave("../Manuscript/figures/neighbours.png", p, width = 12, height = 8, dpi = 700, bg = "white")


```


## Random extinction

So we know how well ace recovers the disparity value of the whole morphospace... but how about the performance of ace recovering true disparity changes across time bins? 
Often, people look at disparity across major evolutionary events eg. a mass extinction.
In this case, I simulated a series of mass extinction events (80% severity).
I looked at disparity changes pre and post extinction (10Ma before after.)


```{R echo = FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
matched_strict <- readRDS("../Data/matrices/randomExtinction/matched.strict.Rds")
matched_rel <- readRDS("../Data/matrices/randomExtinction/matched.rel.Rds")
matched_sample <- readRDS( "../Data/matrices/randomExtinction/matched.sample.Rds")
matched_no_ace <- readRDS( "../Data/matrices/randomExtinction/matched.no_ace.Rds")


strict_df <- data.frame(
  ace = matched_strict$ace,
  true = matched_strict$true,
  fossil_level = as.factor(matched_strict$fossil_level)
)

# Heat colors (red = high preservation, yellow = low)
library(viridis)
fossil_colors <- viridis(5, option = "plasma", direction = -1)
names(fossil_colors) <- c("all", "fossil_high", "fossil_med", "fossil_low", "living")



# all_true <- c(matched_rel$true, matched_strict$true, matched_sample$true, matched_no_ace$true)
all_ace <- c(matched_rel$ace, matched_strict$ace, matched_sample$ace, matched_no_ace$ace)




sample_fit <- lm(matched_sample$ace ~ matched_sample$true)
no_ace_fit <- lm(matched_no_ace$ace ~ matched_no_ace$true)

summary(sample_fit)

png("../Manuscript/figures/randomExtinction/no_ace_disp_change.png", width = 7, height = 7, units = "in", bg = "white")

# Create correlation plot
# Create the plot
plot(matched_sample$true, matched_sample$ace,
     xlab = "True Disparity Change",
     ylab = "ACED Disparity Change",  # Fixed label
     main = "Disparity Change: Sample ACE vs True",
     pch = 16, 
     col = fossil_colors[matched_sample$fossil_level],
     cex = 1,
     xlim = range(matched_sample$true),
     ylim = range(all_ace))

# Add 1:1 line
abline(0, 1, col = "black", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
legend("topleft", 
       legend = names(fossil_colors),
       col = fossil_colors,
       pch = 16,
       cex = 1,
       title = "Fossil Level")

dev.off()


## Note that for living no ace - there is no way that living taxa can be present during the mass extinction so it cannot be compared.


# Create the plot
plot(matched_no_ace$true, matched_no_ace$ace,
     xlab = "True Disparity Change",
     ylab = "ACED Disparity Change",  # Fixed label
     main = "Disparity Change: No ACE vs True",
     pch = 16, 
     col = fossil_colors[matched_no_ace$fossil_level],
     cex = 1,
     xlim = range(matched_rel$true),
     ylim = range(all_ace))

# Add 1:1 line
abline(0, 1, col = "black", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
legend("topleft", 
       legend = names(fossil_colors),
       col = fossil_colors,
       pch = 16,
       cex = 1,
       title = "Fossil Level")





```

Key takeaways:

* Fossil sampling increases performance of ace recovering true disparity change drastically; some estimates (with low fossil sampling) are over by >100% (and in the wrong direction!)
* In general, disparity does not change across a random mass extinction, with the distribution of changes in disparity centred around 0.
* Using ancestral state estimation generally performs better than no ancestral state estimation for finding the true disparity change (but hard to tell, in this case I looked at the R^2 for model fit.)



## Selective extinction

How about in a selective mass extinction?
Here, we might expect ace to perform worse, since the trait distributions post extinction are biased by selectivity.
In this case, I simulated that anything with trait values 0 or 1 across 5 correlated and 2 independent traits has a probability of 0.9 to go extinct.
Therefore, trait values post extinction are biased towards 2, rather than 0/1.

### Sample ace selective extinction

```{R echo = FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
matched_strict <- readRDS("../Data/matrices/selectiveExtinction/matched.strict.Rds")
matched_rel <- readRDS("../Data/matrices/selectiveExtinction/matched.rel.Rds")
matched_sample <- readRDS( "../Data/matrices/selectiveExtinction/matched.sample.Rds")
matched_no_ace <- readRDS( "../Data/matrices/selectiveExtinction/matched.no_ace.Rds")




# Heat colors (red = high preservation, yellow = low)
library(viridis)
fossil_colors <- viridis(5, option = "plasma", direction = -1)
names(fossil_colors) <- c("all", "fossil_high", "fossil_med", "fossil_low", "living")


# # Terrain colors
# fossil_colors <- terrain.colors(5)
# names(fossil_colors) <- c("all", "fossil_high", "fossil_med", "fossil_low", "living")

# all_true <- c(matched_rel$true, matched_strict$true, matched_sample$true, matched_no_ace$true)
all_ace <- c(matched_rel$ace, matched_strict$ace, matched_sample$ace, matched_no_ace$ace)



# Create correlation plot
# Create the plot




# Create correlation plot
# Create the plot



# # Create correlation plot
# # Create the plot
# plot(matched_sample$true, matched_sample$ace,
#      xlab = "True Disparity Change",
#      ylab = "ACED Disparity Change",  # Fixed label
#      main = "Disparity Change: Sample ACE vs True",
#      pch = 16, 
#      col = fossil_colors[matched_sample$fossil_level],
#      cex = 1,
#      xlim = range(matched_rel$true),
#      ylim = range(all_ace))

# # Add 1:1 line
# abline(0, 1, col = "black", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
# legend("topleft", 
#        legend = names(fossil_colors),
#        col = fossil_colors,
#        pch = 16,
#        cex = 1,
#        title = "Fossil Level")


# ## Note that for living no ace - there is no way that living taxa can be present during the mass extinction so it cannot be compared.


# # Create the plot
# plot(matched_no_ace$true, matched_no_ace$ace,
#      xlab = "True Disparity Change",
#      ylab = "ACED Disparity Change",  # Fixed label
#      main = "Disparity Change: No ACE vs True",
#      pch = 16, 
#      col = fossil_colors[matched_no_ace$fossil_level],
#      cex = 1,
#      xlim = range(matched_rel$true),
#      ylim = range(all_ace))

# # Add 1:1 line
# abline(0, 1, col = "black", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
# legend("topleft", 
#        legend = names(fossil_colors),
#        col = fossil_colors,
#        pch = 16,
#        cex = 1,
#        title = "Fossil Level")





knitr::include_graphics(path = "../Manuscript/figures/selectiveExtinction/correlation/sample_disp_change.png")
```


### No ace selective extinction

```{r echo = FALSE}
knitr::include_graphics(path = "../Manuscript/figures/selectiveExtinction/correlation/no_ace_disp_change.png")


```

Key takeaways:
 
* small sample size (at the moment), but no ace in this case has a better model fit than with ace: suggests that selective extinctions can confound ace causing error.
* however, the error is not major and the sample method still recovers true disparity change with relatively high accuracy (with high fossil sampling).

# Key takeaway from whole paper

* Propagating uncertainty of ancestral state estimations down macroevolutionary pipelines (such as disparity) is key for recovering true signals.
* Sample as many fossils as you can - even though the overall disparity signal can be recovered with low fossil sampling level, this is not true when looking at disparity through time. 
* If you have super fast transition rates, be cautious of ace... fit an Mk model prior to doing ace to see how fast your rates are?? Or only perform ancestral state estimation on slow evolving traits?? Can discuss these take home messages?
